title: Purpose-based Access Controls at Palantir (Palantir Explained, #2)
summary: Palantir's official blog post explaining Purpose-Based Access Control (PBAC), where users apply for access to a Purpose rather than individual datasets. Data governance teams scope data to purposes and must record rationale for every access decision, enabling auditors to understand not just who has access but why.
relevantParts: Defines PBAC as the core governance model where access is granted to purposes, not individuals
Explains rationale recording requirements for governance decisions
Describes how purpose owners strengthen accountability and prevent ungoverned dark areas

title: Overview - Security - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: The main security overview page for Palantir Foundry. Describes the combination of mandatory and discretionary controls, with mandatory controls propagating via provenance and lineage, discretionary permissions via roles, and granular row/column-level controls based on user attributes. Covers encryption, SSO, MFA, and audit logging.
relevantParts: Defines the dual mandatory/discretionary control architecture
Lists core security features: encryption, SSO, MFA, authorization, audit logging
Explains how controls achieve flexible granularity from project-level to cell-level

title: Concepts - Markings - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Markings, Foundry's implementation of mandatory access controls. Markings define eligibility criteria that restrict visibility and actions, propagate through data lineage and file hierarchy, and travel with the data unlike role-based access. Removing markings requires the Expand Access permission on the marking itself.
relevantParts: Markings are mandatory controls that propagate through data lineage
Markings travel with data, unlike role-based access which is location-based
Removing markings requires special Expand Access permission, independent of Owner role

title: Concepts - Classification-based Access Controls - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Classification-Based Access Controls (CBAC), mandatory controls for protecting sensitive government information. CBAC markings restrict access hierarchically (e.g., Secret or below). Classification markings are configured in categories, and a classification can comprise markings from different categories.
relevantParts: Defines CBAC as hierarchical classification markings for government data
Classifications can be composed of markings from multiple categories
Mandatory control that restricts access based on user classification level

title: Object permissioning - Ontology permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Ontology permissions, the central permissioning model for Ontology resources. Roles grant access at the Ontology level or individual resource level. Project-based permissions allow ontology resources (object types, action types, link types, interfaces, shared properties) to inherit permissions from their containing project.
relevantParts: Roles are the central permissioning model for the Ontology
Project-based permissions let ontology resources inherit from containing projects
Separates metadata permissions (Ontology Editor) from data permissions (datasource access)

title: Object permissioning - Object security policies - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on object and property security policies that achieve cell-level permissions. Object security policies control visibility of object instances (row-level), while property security policies control visibility of specific property values (column-level). These are independent of backing datasource permissions.
relevantParts: Object security policies = row-level security for object instances
Property security policies = column-level security for specific properties
Policies are independent of backing datasource permissions

title: Concepts - Projects and roles - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Projects and Roles as the foundation of discretionary access control. Projects are the primary security boundary, and roles are sets of operations granted on resources and their children. Custom roles can be created, and role sets are applied at the space level.
relevantParts: Projects are the primary security boundary in Foundry
Roles are sets of operations granted on resources and inherited by children
Custom roles can be created for different user types

title: Security auditing - Audit logs - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Foundry's comprehensive audit logging system. Audit logs capture who performed an action (identity), what the action was (categorized), when it happened (timestamps), and where (resources/systems). Logs are delivered in audit.2 and audit.3 schemas with append-only infrastructure for integrity.
relevantParts: Comprehensive audit trail answering who/what/when/where for every action
Append-only infrastructure ensures audit trail integrity
External SIEM integration via audit API with 15-minute availability

title: Security auditing - Audit log categories - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation categorizing the types of audit events captured in Foundry. Audit logs are organized by category and action type, enabling security teams to detect threats, investigate incidents, ensure compliance, and maintain accountability across the platform.
relevantParts: Audit events are categorized by type and intent
Supports compliance and threat detection use cases
Structured categorization enables efficient SIEM filtering

title: The Next Generation of Audit Logging at Palantir - Palantir Blog
summary: Palantir blog post on the evolution of their audit logging system. Describes the engineering challenges of building trust-at-scale audit infrastructure, the transition to new audit log schemas, and how audit logs serve as the foundation of Palantir's security and governance architecture.
relevantParts: Engineering perspective on building scalable audit infrastructure
Describes the audit.3 schema evolution
Positions audit logs as unsung hero of security and governance architecture

title: Security auditing - Monitor audit logs - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on monitoring audit logs in Foundry. Covers how to export audit logs to datasets for analysis, integrate with external SIEMs, and set up monitoring workflows for security operations teams.
relevantParts: Export audit logs to Foundry datasets for in-platform analysis
SIEM integration for external security monitoring
Operational monitoring workflow guidance

title: Data protection and governance - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation covering data governance across the entire lifecycle from ingestion to deletion. Describes how data protection and governance integrate with Foundry's access controls, including purpose-based access, markings, organizations, restricted views, and the Cipher encryption service.
relevantParts: Lifecycle approach to data governance from ingestion to deletion
Integration of PBAC, markings, organizations, and Cipher
Tools for managing sensitive/personal data compliance

title: Building Software for a Zero Trust World - Palantir Blog
summary: Palantir's blog post on implementing zero trust architecture across their corporate environment and platforms. Describes how all requests are treated as untrusted until authenticated, CI enforcement of authenticated endpoints, and infrastructure-as-code security checks.
relevantParts: Zero trust: all requests untrusted until explicitly authenticated
CI pipelines reject unauthenticated endpoints
Infrastructure-as-code with security checks in change management

title: Shared security responsibility model - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on the shared responsibility model between Palantir and customers. Palantir and IaaS providers secure physical security, host infrastructure, networking, and application security, while customers maintain responsibility for what they host in Foundry and their access control configurations.
relevantParts: Delineates security responsibilities between Palantir and customers
Palantir handles infrastructure and application-level security
Customers responsible for their data, access grants, and configurations

title: Concepts - Organizations and spaces - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Organizations as mandatory access controls that enforce strict silos between groups of users and resources. Users belong to one organization but can guest-access others. Organizations are inherited via file hierarchy and direct dependencies, functioning as mandatory controls.
relevantParts: Organizations enforce strict data silos as mandatory controls
Users belong to one org with optional guest access to others
Organizations propagate via file hierarchy and data dependencies

title: Concepts - Restricted views - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Restricted Views that limit dataset access to only rows a user has permission to see. Restricted views use granular policies based on user attributes, group memberships, and data values to filter rows. They are read-only to prevent schema alterations that could expose restricted data.
relevantParts: Restricted views enforce row-level security based on user attributes
Read-only to prevent schema changes that could leak restricted data
Granular policies compare user attributes against data columns

title: Management - Manage granular policies - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on configuring granular policies for row-level security. Policies are templates that compare user attributes, group memberships, and data values. When data is requested, the template converts into a query returning only permitted rows/objects for that user.
relevantParts: Granular policies are templates filled with user attributes at query time
Support comparisons between user attributes, columns, and static values
Used by both restricted views and object security policies

title: Action types - Permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Action Type permissions, covering who can configure, view, and execute actions. Action type configuration does not display permission settings on affected underlying object types, requiring the configurator to verify permissions independently.
relevantParts: Action type permissions are separate from object type permissions
Configurators must independently verify underlying object type permissions
Controls who can configure, view, and execute actions

title: Action types - Submission criteria - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on submission criteria (formerly validations) that determine whether an action can be submitted. Criteria combine conditions based on user context, parameters, and static information into logical statements. Supports encoding business logic into data editing permissions for Ontology data quality.
relevantParts: Submission criteria encode business logic into action permissions
Conditions combine user context, parameters, and static values
Independent from permissions governing action type editing

title: Action types - Parameters - Object dropdown security considerations - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on security considerations for object dropdown parameters in actions. Static value filters in dropdowns risk exposing property value combinations to unauthorized users. Mitigation involves using object properties or parameters rather than static filters.
relevantParts: Static filters in dropdowns can expose data to unauthorized users
Object property-based filters are more secure than static values
Security risk in action parameter configuration

title: Action types - Action log - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on the Action Log, which records every action execution in the Ontology. Provides audit trail for all data modifications performed through actions, supporting compliance and governance requirements.
relevantParts: Records every action execution for audit purposes
Supports compliance by tracking data modifications
Part of the broader Ontology audit infrastructure

title: Third-party applications - Writing OAuth2 clients for Foundry - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on OAuth2 client implementation for Foundry, covering Authorization Code flow (user-context) and Client Credentials grant (service user). Refresh tokens rotate on each use, with reuse detection invalidating all tokens from the grant. Inactive tokens expire after 30 days.
relevantParts: OAuth2 Authorization Code flow for user-context access
Client Credentials grant for service user authentication
Refresh token rotation and reuse detection for compromised token mitigation

title: Authentication - API Reference - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: API reference for Foundry authentication. All APIs use OAuth 2.0 with bearer tokens passed as Authorization headers. Covers token generation, management, and security best practices including never hardcoding tokens or sharing them publicly.
relevantParts: All Foundry APIs authenticate via OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens
Bearer tokens passed as Authorization headers
Security best practices for token management

title: Protecting tokens and API keys - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Security guidance on protecting tokens and API keys in Foundry. Covers best practices for token storage, rotation, and access control. Emphasizes never sharing tokens publicly or with other users and not hardcoding them in scripts.
relevantParts: Best practices for secure token storage and rotation
Never share tokens publicly or hardcode in scripts
Guidance on API key management

title: Protecting identity - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on SSO security in Foundry, covering SAML 2.0 and OIDC 1.0 integration with identity providers. Supports passwordless login via FIDO2 passkeys, provider group mirroring, and JIT user provisioning. Azure AD Conditional Access used as Policy Decision Point.
relevantParts: SAML 2.0 and OIDC 1.0 for identity provider integration
FIDO2 passkeys for passwordless authentication
Provider group mirroring synchronizes IdP groups with Foundry

title: Authentication - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Overview of Foundry authentication architecture. Access is managed by identity providers that validate users and provide user information, attributes, and groups. Covers supported authentication methods including IdP integration, passwordless, service user authentication, and MFA.
relevantParts: Identity providers validate users and provide attributes/groups
Multiple authentication methods: IdP, passwordless, service users
MFA support for enhanced identity protection

title: Getting started - Requesting justification for sensitive actions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on configuring justification prompts for sensitive actions. Checkpoints interrupt users with prompts requiring justification before performing sensitive operations. Justification types include acknowledgment, free-text response, dropdown selection, and reauthentication.
relevantParts: Checkpoints interrupt sensitive actions to require justification
Four justification types: acknowledgment, response, dropdown, reauthentication
All justifications are saved for administrative review

title: Checkpoints - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Overview of the Checkpoints governance tool that supports purpose justification and auditability. Checkpoints interrupt potentially sensitive user interactions with configurable prompts, linking to data handling policies and requiring justifications that are saved for audit review.
relevantParts: Configurable prompts for sensitive actions like data export
Links to organizational data handling policies
Rich contextual history for administrators reviewing sensitive actions

title: Cipher - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Overview of Cipher, Foundry's data obfuscation service using cryptographic operations. Supports AES-GCM-SIV probabilistic encryption, AES-SIV deterministic encryption, SHA-256/512 hashing with pepper, and visual obfuscation for images. Managed through Channels and Licenses for key and permission management.
relevantParts: Multiple encryption algorithms: AES-GCM-SIV, AES-SIV, SHA-256/512
Visual obfuscation for image PII/PHI with reversible scrambling
Channels and Licenses model for key and permission management

title: Cipher - Core concepts - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Core concepts documentation for Cipher covering Channels (algorithm + key configuration), Licenses (permission grants for crypto operations), and the distinction between probabilistic and deterministic encryption. Deterministic encryption preserves join capability while probabilistic provides stronger security.
relevantParts: Channels define algorithm and key configuration
Licenses control who can perform which crypto operations
Deterministic vs probabilistic encryption trade-offs

title: Security glossary - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Comprehensive glossary of security terms used in Foundry. Defines key concepts including Access (visibility of resource existence), Mandatory Controls (all-or-nothing restrictions like markings and organizations), Discretionary Controls (capabilities via roles), and the relationship between them.
relevantParts: Formal definitions of Access, Mandatory Controls, Discretionary Controls
Distinction between access (knowing resource exists) and capabilities (roles)
Central reference for all Foundry security terminology

title: AIP security and privacy - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform) security. AIP inherits all of Foundry's security controls including granular access controls, encryption, and auditing. AI guardrails govern every aspect of the system, and AIP Agent Studio grants LLMs access only to what is necessary for each task.
relevantParts: AIP inherits Foundry's full security model
Granular AI guardrails govern LLM access to data
Agent Studio applies least-privilege principle to AI operations

title: How Palantir Meets IL6 Security Requirements with Apollo - Palantir Blog
summary: Blog post on how Apollo meets DoD IL6 security requirements. All artifacts are cryptographically signed with integrity validation. Apollo manages vulnerability detection, alerting teams and using severity criteria to gate deployments. Supports disconnected networks and air-gapped environments.
relevantParts: Cryptographic signing and integrity validation for all deployment artifacts
Vulnerability severity gates for deployment decisions
Support for air-gapped and disconnected network deployments

title: Security - Authorization - Palantir Apollo Documentation
summary: Documentation on Apollo's authorization model. Apollo uses role-based access controls (RBAC) where Teams gain permissions based on assigned roles. Configuration and secrets are decoupled from infrastructure, enabling secure reapplication onto new systems.
relevantParts: Apollo uses RBAC with Teams and assigned roles
Secrets decoupled from infrastructure for secure management
Role-based operations control for deployment actions

title: Data Lineage - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Data Lineage, Foundry's interactive tool for understanding how data flows through the platform. Captures integration history including time, source, and revision. Pipeline mode compute modules track all data inputs/outputs through the build system for provenance control.
relevantParts: Tracks data flow and transformation history across the platform
Pipeline mode enforces strict provenance control via build system
Supports security through marking propagation along lineage

title: Object and link types - Properties - Mandatory control properties - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on mandatory control properties in the Ontology. These properties carry mandatory access controls (markings and organizations) at the object/property level, enabling fine-grained mandatory controls on Ontology data that propagate with the data.
relevantParts: Mandatory control properties carry markings/organizations at the object level
Enables fine-grained mandatory controls on Ontology properties
Controls propagate with data through the system

title: Palantir Granted FedRAMP High Baseline Authorization
summary: Investor relations announcement that Palantir received FedRAMP High Authorization for its Federal Cloud Service, enabling delivery of AIP, Apollo, Foundry, and Gotham to the U.S. Government with highest security standards. Part of a portfolio including IL5, IL6, and CMMC Level 2.
relevantParts: FedRAMP High covers all Palantir products: AIP, Apollo, Foundry, Gotham
One of only six cloud service providers with IL6 accreditation
Comprehensive federal security authorization portfolio

title: Palantir Technologies Achieves Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) Level 2
summary: Press release announcing Palantir's CMMC Level 2 certification, confirming implementation of NIST SP 800-171 security requirements for protecting sensitive government data. Builds upon existing FedRAMP High, IL5, and IL6 authorizations.
relevantParts: CMMC Level 2 = NIST SP 800-171 implementation
Validates ability to protect Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI)
Adds to existing FedRAMP High, IL5, IL6 portfolio

title: Object permissioning - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Overview of object permissioning in the Foundry Ontology. Describes how granular, robust, and flexible security controls apply to all ontology entities including object types, link types, and action types. Security is built into the Ontology itself, so application builders need not account for security requirements separately.
relevantParts: Security is resident in the Ontology, not in individual applications
Covers object types, link types, action types, and their instances
Application builders get elevated security without explicit implementation

title: Object permissioning - Managing object security - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on managing object security including datasource-based and Ontology-based approaches. Object and property policies support cell-level security, and object security can be configured directly in the Ontology Manager view. Covers multi-datasource object types for column-level security.
relevantParts: Datasource-based vs Ontology-based security approaches
Cell-level security through combined row and column policies
Column-wise MDOs enable column-level access controls

title: Ontology Manager - Ontology roles migration - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on migrating from legacy datasource-derived permissions to Ontology roles. Describes how Ontology roles replaced the old authorization model, applying to object types, link types, and Action types as Ontology resources with dedicated Editor and Owner role types.
relevantParts: Migration path from datasource-derived permissions to Ontology roles
Ontology roles are the default authorization model for Ontology resources
Separate Ontology Editor role that controls metadata without data access

title: Ontology SDK - Permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on permissions within the Ontology SDK. Covers how applications choose between User permissions (Authorization Code OAuth) and Application permissions (Client Credentials OAuth), and how resource access scopes are managed when adding object types, link types, and functions to SDK configuration.
relevantParts: Two permission models: User permissions and Application permissions
Resource access scopes auto-managed when adding Ontology resources
Check access panel for verifying user permissions on applications

title: Functions - Language agnostic features - Permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on permissions for Functions in the Ontology. Functions run with specific permission contexts that determine what data they can access, ensuring that function execution respects the caller's access level and security policies.
relevantParts: Functions execute within permission contexts
Function access respects caller's security policies
Language-agnostic permission model for all function types

title: Palantir MCP - Security - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on security for Palantir's Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration. Covers how MCP connections to Foundry inherit the platform's security model, ensuring AI tools and external integrations respect existing access controls and audit requirements.
relevantParts: MCP integrations inherit Foundry's security model
AI tool connections subject to existing access controls
External integration security for AI assistants

title: Palantir Privacy and Governance Whitepaper
summary: Official Palantir whitepaper covering privacy and governance architecture. Describes purpose-based access controls, data lineage tracking, Cipher encryption, Checkpoints justification, and the overall governance framework. Positions Palantir's security investments as competitive advantages for customer success.
relevantParts: Comprehensive treatment of privacy and governance across all features
Links PBAC, lineage, Cipher, and Checkpoints into unified framework
Official Palantir positioning on governance architecture

title: Operational Security: Enabling transparency, collaboration, and privacy - Palantir Blog
summary: Blog post on operational security in Palantir's RFX series. Discusses how Palantir balances transparency and collaboration with privacy through purpose-based access controls, describing the operational aspects of their security model.
relevantParts: Balances transparency/collaboration with privacy requirements
Operational perspective on PBAC implementation
Part of Palantir's RFX blog series on platform capabilities

title: Trust in Data (Palantir Explained #4) - Palantir Blog
summary: Palantir blog post explaining how data trust is built through provenance tracking, lineage, and audit trails. Part of the Palantir Explained series. Describes how tracking data from source through all transformations enables users to assess data reliability and security.
relevantParts: Data trust through comprehensive provenance and lineage tracking
Source-to-consumption traceability for all data
Foundational concept for Palantir's security architecture

title: AI Systems Governance through the Palantir Platform - Palantir Blog
summary: Blog post on AI governance within the Palantir platform. Describes how Palantir's security and governance tools extend to AI/ML systems, ensuring AI operations are auditable, governed, and compliant with organizational policies.
relevantParts: Extends governance framework to AI/ML operations
AI system auditability and compliance
Organizational policy enforcement for AI

title: Getting started - Protecting sensitive data - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Getting started guide for protecting sensitive data in Foundry. Provides a practical overview of combining markings, organizations, restricted views, Cipher, and Checkpoints to build a comprehensive data protection strategy for sensitive data types like PII and PHI.
relevantParts: Practical guide combining all security primitives for data protection
Covers PII, PHI, and other sensitive data classifications
Starting point for implementing comprehensive data protection

title: Download controls - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on download controls in Foundry that restrict the ability to export data from the platform. Works in conjunction with Checkpoints to require justification for data downloads, preventing unauthorized data exfiltration.
relevantParts: Controls for restricting data export from Foundry
Integration with Checkpoints for justified downloads
Prevention of unauthorized data exfiltration

title: Management - Manage roles - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on managing roles in Foundry. Roles are sets of operations that grant specific capabilities. Covers creating custom roles, role sets applied at the space level, default role types (Owner, Editor, Viewer), and group-based permission management.
relevantParts: Custom role creation for different user types
Role sets applied at space level for uniform management
Group-based permission management reduces individual role clutter

title: Protecting your self-hosted Foundry installation - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on securing self-hosted Foundry deployments. Covers infrastructure security requirements including network isolation, encryption configuration, HSM recommendations for key management, and TLS 1.2+ with strong cipher suites.
relevantParts: Self-hosted deployment security requirements
HSM recommendations for cryptographic key storage
TLS 1.2+ and AES-256/128 encryption standards

title: Why you should govern data access through Purpose-Based Access Control - Dataminded on Medium
summary: Third-party analysis of Purpose-Based Access Control as a governance strategy, referencing Palantir's implementation. Explains how PBAC is simpler and more agile than traditional access management while maintaining compliance with regulatory requirements.
relevantParts: Independent analysis of PBAC as governance pattern
Comparison with traditional access management approaches
Regulatory compliance benefits of PBAC

title: Concepts - Users and groups - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on user and group management in Foundry. Users are authenticated via identity providers and assigned to groups for permission management. Groups can be synchronized from IdP (provider groups) or managed within Foundry, enabling scalable access control.
relevantParts: Users authenticated via external identity providers
Groups enable scalable permission management
Provider groups synchronize from IdP for consistency

title: Organization settings - Configure application access - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on configuring application-level access controls within Foundry. Covers how organization administrators can control which applications are available to users, managing the surface area of platform functionality accessible to different user groups.
relevantParts: Organization-level application access controls
Administrators control available platform functionality
Restricts application surface area per user group

title: How Palantir Enables a Secure, Rapid Software Development Environment - Palantir Blog
summary: Blog post on Palantir's secure software development practices. Describes how Palantir uses zero trust, CI/CD security checks, Microsoft Azure AD Conditional Access, and device-based authentication policies to secure their development pipeline.
relevantParts: Azure AD Conditional Access as Policy Decision Point
Device-based authentication guarantees
CI/CD pipelines enforce security in development workflow

title: Object permissioning - Multi-datasource object types (MDOs) - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on multi-datasource objects (MDOs) that enable column-level security through column-wise joins across different datasources. Distinct subsets of properties can come from different datasources with different access controls, enabling fine-grained column-level access.
relevantParts: Column-wise MDOs enable column-level access controls
Different property subsets from different datasources with different permissions
Supports use cases requiring segregated column access

title: palantir/conjure - Strongly typed HTTP/JSON APIs for browsers and microservices
summary: GitHub repository for Conjure, Palantir's open-source toolchain for defining strongly-typed HTTP/JSON APIs. Used internally for Palantir's API security model, providing type-safe client/server interfaces that enforce contract compliance at the API boundary.
relevantParts: Type-safe API boundary enforcement
Used for Palantir's internal API security model
Contract compliance at the API layer

title: Authentication - Multi-factor authentication - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on multi-factor authentication (MFA) configuration in Foundry. Provides guidance on enforcing MFA for user authentication to add an additional layer of identity verification beyond passwords or SSO.
relevantParts: MFA enforcement configuration
Additional identity verification layer
Complements SSO and passwordless authentication

title: Use case patterns - Multi-organization ecosystems - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on multi-organization ecosystem patterns in Foundry. Describes how multiple organizations can coexist within an enrollment with strict data isolation, supporting use cases like government inter-agency collaboration or enterprise conglomerate data sharing.
relevantParts: Multi-organization patterns for inter-agency or inter-company collaboration
Strict data isolation between organizations within same enrollment
Guest membership model for controlled cross-organization access

title: AI ethics and governance - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on AI ethics and governance within Palantir's AIP platform. Covers how governance tools maintain accountability and historical lineage in AI operations, ensuring AI systems operate within defined boundaries with transparency and auditability.
relevantParts: AI governance with accountability and lineage tracking
Defined operational boundaries for AI systems
Transparency and auditability for AI decisions

title: Compute modules - Configuration - Execution modes - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on compute module execution modes. Pipeline mode enforces strict provenance control and security via the Foundry build system. All inputs/outputs must pass through the build system, maintaining data integrity and traceability. Compute modules operate under zero trust security model.
relevantParts: Pipeline mode enforces strict provenance control
Zero trust security model for compute modules
No external network access by default for maximum isolation

title: Security and Privacy on Palantir: Best Practices - Bronson.AI
summary: Third-party guide on Palantir security and privacy best practices. Covers encryption (TLS 1.2+, AES-256/128), compliance with HIPAA, GDPR, ITAR frameworks, and practical recommendations for configuring Foundry's security controls.
relevantParts: Third-party perspective on Palantir security best practices
Encryption standards: TLS 1.2+, AES-256/128
Compliance framework alignment: HIPAA, GDPR, ITAR

title: Third-party applications - Foundry third-party application & API guidance - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on security guidance for third-party applications integrating with Foundry. Covers OAuth2 authentication, API security requirements, and how third-party applications interact with Foundry's security model including token management and scope restrictions.
relevantParts: Third-party application security requirements
OAuth2 integration guidance for external apps
Scope restrictions and token management for APIs

title: Ontology Manager - Migrate to project-based permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on migrating Ontology resources to project-based permissions. This new model allows object types, action types, link types, interfaces, and shared properties to be saved within projects and inherit the project's permission structure for unified management.
relevantParts: Project-based permissions unify Ontology resource management
All core Ontology types (object, action, link, interface, shared property) supported
Inheritance model simplifies permission administration

title: Palantir FedStart
summary: Product page for Palantir FedStart, a program that enables SaaS companies to deliver products to government within Palantir's accredited environment. Products run in Palantir's FedRAMP High and IL5 accredited infrastructure without requiring separate security authorizations.
relevantParts: FedStart leverages Palantir's existing security accreditations
SaaS products inherit FedRAMP High and IL5 compliance
Reduces time-to-government-deployment through shared accreditation

title: Data Connection - Listeners - Security - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on security for Data Connection Listeners, which allow external systems to push data into Foundry. Listeners can be mounted to dedicated subdomains for granular ingress control, governance workflows, and isolation of less secure endpoints from primary enrollment domains.
relevantParts: Dedicated subdomains for granular ingress control
Isolation of less secure endpoints from primary domains
Governance workflows for data ingestion security

title: Palantir Privacy and Security Statement
summary: Palantir's official privacy and security statement. Describes the company's commitment to data protection, the instance-per-client deployment model ensuring data isolation, and how operational/technical/procedural safeguards keep customer environments separate, sovereign, and secure.
relevantParts: Instance-per-client model ensures complete data isolation
Operational, technical, and procedural safeguards for sovereignty
Corporate-level commitment to data protection
